preFix.title: Cutting a bridle joint
preFix.subtitle: A saw-and-chisel joint for joining timber at a T or corner, without specialist machinery
preFix.excerpt: How to cut a bridle joint by hand. The bridle is a strong, visible joint used in frame construction, a notched slot on one timber receives a tenon cut from the other. Covers marking out, sawing, and fitting the joint.
preFix.sourceNotes: Newey & Drage, Practical Carpentry and Joinery (c.1905). Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

content.text: The
bridle joint

sits between a mortise-and-tenon and a simple halving joint in both difficulty and strength. It is the standard joint for gate frames, simple trestle tops, and any structural frame where the joint will be visible and the width of the slot can be used aesthetically. Cutting it requires a marked-out gauge line, clean saw cuts on the waste side of the line, and careful paring with a sharp chisel.
When to use it

At T-junctions and corner junctions in frame construction where both faces of the joint are visible, a gate frame, a workbench top rail, a stool leg. Where the joint will be hidden (inside a door frame or wall structure), a mortise and tenon is stronger; the bridle does not hold as well against twisting forces because the slot is open.
What you need
Two pieces of timber of the same cross-section (or close to it). The timber must be square in section, twisted or bowed stock produces a joint that cannot be fitted without gaps. Set the
marking gauge
to exactly one-third of the timber width before starting. Check the chisel edge against the oilstone before cutting the joint, a dull chisel tears the slot walls.
Step 1, Mark the slot

On the slotted piece (the T-bar of the T-junction), mark the shoulder depth, the depth the tenon will enter, with the try square and pencil. Mark the shoulder line all the way around the timber. Set the gauge to one-third of the width and scribe a line across the face and edges from the shoulder line to the end. This is the slot width: the outer third and the inner third are removed, leaving the middle third as the wall.
Step 2, Mark the tenon

On the tenon piece, scribe the shoulder line at the same depth with the try square. Set the gauge to the same one-third setting and scribe both cheeks of the tenon from the shoulder to the end. The tenon thickness, the middle third, must match the slot width exactly. Compare the two gauged pieces directly before sawing.
Step 3, Saw the slot

Clamp the slotted piece upright. Saw along the gauge lines on the waste side of the line, inside the lines, not on them. A saw cut on the line takes the joint from a tight fit to a loose one. Saw to the shoulder line on both sides. Remove the waste between the saw cuts with the chisel and wooden mallet: work from each face toward the centre, paring horizontally until the bottom of the slot is level and smooth.
Step 4, Saw the tenon

Saw along the cheek lines of the tenon on the waste side. Saw both cheeks, then saw across the shoulder line to remove the waste pieces. The tenon cheeks should be smooth from the saw; if the saw has wandered, pare back to the gauge line with the chisel held bevel-down, taking thin shavings.
Step 5, Fit the joint

Test the fit dry. The tenon should enter the slot with hand pressure only, no mallet needed on a dry fit. A joint that needs forcing will split when glued, because the glue adds a small amount of thickness. If the joint is tight, pare thin shavings from the tenon cheeks until it fits. If the joint is loose, the only fix is new timber.
What it looks like when it's right
The tenon enters the slot flush on both faces with the timber it joins, leaves no gap at the shoulder line, and does not rock or twist in the slot. The slot walls are smooth with no torn grain.
Common mistakes
Sawing on the line instead of to the waste side: removes the precise one-third thickness and leaves a loose fit. Always saw on the waste side, the chisel takes the final fitting.
Slot bottom not flat: the tenon rocks in the slot. Pare the bottom level with the chisel held flat, testing with a small steel rule.
Not checking that both timbers are square in section: a bridle joint in twisted timber cannot pull square however carefully it is cut.
